title: Why the return of tears after years of numbness is a breakthrough
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
You've been waiting for this moment your whole life

I remember the exact moment it happened. I was sitting on my bathroom floor, staring at a half-empty bottle of shampoo, and then something cracked. A sound came out of me I hadn't heard in years. A sob. Wet, ugly, and completely uninvited. I sat there for twenty minutes crying about nothing and everything. About the shampoo. About my childhood. About the years I spent feeling like a ghost in my own body.

Here's the thing. For a long time, I thought my inability to cry was a strength. I thought it meant I was tough, that I had conquered my pain. I'd watch movies where characters wept and feel a strange sense of superiority. Look at them, so fragile. I don't need that release. I'm above it.

I wasn't above it. I was disconnected from it.

When you've lived through complex trauma, especially early and repeated trauma, your nervous system does something and terrifying. It learns to protect you by shutting down the very channels that would let you feel. Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, calls this the "freeze" response. Your body decides that feeling is too dangerous, too overwhelming. So it numbs you. It turns down the volume on your life until everything sounds like it's coming from underwater.

And here's the cruel irony. That numbness, that protective armor you built to survive, becomes its own kind of prison. You can't feel the bad stuff, sure. But you also can't feel the good stuff. You can't feel joy, connection, or the simple pleasure of a warm hug. You're safe, but you're not alive.

So when the tears finally come back, when your body decides it's safe enough to let you feel again, it feels like a betrayal. It feels like weakness. Like you're falling apart. Right?

I'm here to tell you it's the opposite. The return of tears after years of numbness is a breakthrough. It's your nervous system saying, I trust you now. I trust you can handle this.

What numbness actually is

Let's get clear on what we're talking about. Numbness isn't just not crying. It's a full-body experience. It's the hollow feeling in your chest when something sad happens. It's the inability to feel anger when someone hurts you. It's watching your own life from a distance, like you're a character in a movie you don't care about.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. He found that people with complex trauma histories often have a diminished capacity to feel their own emotions. Their brains have learned to dissociate as a survival strategy. When a child is abused or neglected, the brain says, This is too much. I'm going to leave now. And it does. It leaves the body behind.

That's what numbness is. It's your brain protecting you from pain that felt unsurvivable at the time. And here's the hard truth. It worked. That dissociation, that numbness, kept you alive. It got you through things that would have broken a less protected person. You should feel grateful to that part of you that learned to go numb. It saved your life.

But now you're an adult. You're in a different situation. The dangers that required that level of protection are mostly gone. The problem is your nervous system doesn't know that. It's still running the old program. It's still keeping you safe from threats that no longer exist.

And that's why the return of tears feels so disorienting. Your system is updating its software. It's learning a new way to be in the world. And that process is messy, unpredictable, and deeply uncomfortable.

I've worked with clients who describe the first time they cried after years of numbness as feeling like their skin was being peeled off. The sensation was that raw, that intense. They wanted it to stop. They wanted to go back to the safety of nothingness. But staying in that nothingness means staying half-alive.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry. It helps shift the question from "What's wrong with you?" to "What happened to you?" That shift matters. It changes how you see your own numbness.

The body decides when it's ready

You can't force the tears to come. I tried. I sat in meditation, breathing into my chest, trying to manufacture a crying session. It never worked. The body doesn't respond to force. It responds to safety.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, explains this beautifully. He says your nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety and danger. When it detects danger, it activates your sympathetic nervous system (fight or flight) or your dorsal vagal system (freeze or collapse). When it detects safety, it activates your ventral vagal system, which allows for connection, calm, and emotional expression.

The return of tears means your nervous system has detected enough safety to let you feel. It means your body trusts you now. And that trust is built slowly, over time, through consistent acts of self-care and self-compassion.

For me, the tears came back after I started doing gentle nervous system regulation practices. Things like orienting to my environment, noticing my breath, and placing a hand on my heart when I felt overwhelmed. I wasn't trying to cry. I was just trying to be present. And eventually, presence became safe enough for feeling.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She offers simple, accessible exercises for mapping your nervous system and finding your way back to regulation. It's not about forcing anything. It's about creating the conditions for your body to feel safe.

Think about it. When you're in a state of chronic numbness, your body is in a protective freeze. It's holding tension, holding breath, holding everything in. The tears, when they come, are a release of that held energy. They're your body saying, I can let go now. I'm safe.

What to expect when the tears come back

It's not going to be pretty. I want to be honest with you about that. The first few times you cry after years of numbness, it might feel like you're breaking. The tears might come in waves that feel uncontrollable. You might sob for hours over something small. You might cry and not even know why.

This is normal. This is your body releasing years of stored emotion. It's like a dam that's been holding back a river for decades. When it finally breaks, the water doesn't come out in a neat, controlled stream. It floods.

You might feel ashamed. You might want to stop it. You might judge yourself for being weak. Stay with me here. That judgment is just another protective part of you, a part that learned that showing emotion was dangerous. Thank that part for trying to keep you safe, and then let yourself cry anyway.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who integrates Internal Family Systems with trauma treatment, talks about this process. She says that when we start to unfreeze, we often encounter parts of ourselves that are terrified of feeling. Those parts need to be heard and comforted, not overridden. You can say to that scared part, I know you're afraid. But I'm here now. I can handle this.

And you can. You really can. Because you're not that helpless child anymore. You're an adult with resources, with choices, with the ability to reach out for help.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. Her work on accepting ourselves exactly as we are, including our tears and our numbness, is a lifeline for many of us on this path.

The breakthrough is in the breakdown

I know the title of this article says that the return of tears is a breakthrough. And it is. But I want to be clear about what that breakthrough looks like in real life. It doesn't look like a montage from a movie where someone cries beautifully and then emerges transformed. It looks like snot and swollen eyes and wondering if you'll ever stop. It looks like calling in sick to work because you can't stop crying. It looks like texting your friend at 2 AM saying, I think I'm falling apart.

And that's okay. That's the point.

Falling apart is not the end. It's the beginning of putting yourself back together in a way that's more alive, more present, more connected. You're not losing control. You're gaining access to parts of yourself that have been locked away for years. You're becoming a whole person instead of a protected shell.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your chest tighten? Did your throat close up? Did you feel a wave of relief or a wave of fear? Whatever you felt, it's okay. It's all part of the process.

There's a book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay Gibson. It helped me understand why I learned to go numb in the first place. When your parents can't handle your emotions, you learn to hide them. You learn that feeling is dangerous. That book helped me see that my numbness wasn't a defect. It was an adaptation.

So here's what I want you to take away from this. If the tears are starting to come back, if you feel that crack in the armor, don't panic. Don't try to seal it back up. Let it crack. Let it open. Let yourself feel whatever comes, even if it's messy, even if it's ugly, even if it takes weeks or months to process.

You're not falling apart. You're coming back to life.

And that is the whole point of this work. Not to be happy all the time. Not to be healed in some perfect, tidy way. But to be alive. To feel the full range of what it means to be human. The grief and the joy, the anger and the love, the numbness and the tears.

Wild, right? The thing you thought was your greatest weakness, the tears, turns out to be your greatest sign of progress. The thing you ran from for years is the thing that will set you free.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - A book that many find validates their childhood experience
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
